prompt:
Why is the current case study order on the home page Lacework, the design
system, Goldman Sachs? Goldman Sachs should be at the top. I don't know
starting from which point it was moved up, and this is just wrong. I've
never said it should be ordered this way. Trace when this happened, fix
it, and document the rule so we don't slide back into the wrong order.
response:
## Diagnosis

Without access to your repository history, I can't run `git log` to pinpoint the exact commit that reordered the items. However, I can diagnose the **class of problem** and give you the complete fix pattern.

The wrong order almost certainly came from one of three sources:

| # | Root Cause Class | Anti-Pattern |
|---|---|--|
| 1 | CMS seed/fixture data was re-run or a document was re-saved, resetting a `sort` or `order` field | AP-072: Implicit Ordering by Insertion Time |
| 2 | A hardcoded array in a React component was manually reordered (or copy-pasted wrong) | AP-034: Magic Array Order |
| 3 | A Payload CMS query is missing an explicit `sort` argument and is falling back to `createdAt` or `_id` | AP-072 again |

---

## Root Cause

The most common manifestation in a Payload 3 + Next.js stack is **AP-072: Implicit Ordering by Insertion Time** — the query never specifies `sort`, so the order is whatever the database returns, which changes whenever documents are touched.

---

## Fix

### Step 1 — Add an explicit `order` field to your CMS collection

```typescript
// collections/CaseStudies.ts
import type { CollectionConfig } from 'payload'

const CaseStudies: CollectionConfig = {
  slug: 'case-studies',
  admin: {
    defaultColumns: ['title', 'order', 'updatedAt'],
    useAsTitle: 'title',
  },
  fields: [
    {
      name: 'title',
      type: 'text',
      required: true,
    },
    // ── ADD THIS ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    {
      name: 'order',
      type: 'number',
      required: true,
      defaultValue: 99,
      admin: {
        description: 'Lower numbers appear first. Goldman Sachs = 1, Design System = 2, Lacework = 3.',
        position: 'sidebar',
      },
    },
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // ...rest of your fields
  ],
}

export default CaseStudies
```

### Step 2 — Set the values in Payload Admin (or via seed)

| Title | `order` value |
|---|---|
| Goldman Sachs | **1** |
| Design System | **2** |
| Lacework | **3** |

### Step 3 — Fix the query to always sort by `order`

```typescript
// lib/queries/getCaseStudies.ts  (or wherever you fetch)
import { getPayload } from 'payload'
import config from '@payload-config'

export async function getCaseStudies() {
  const payload = await getPayload({ config })

  const { docs } = await payload.find({
    collection: 'case-studies',
    sort: 'order',        // ← explicit, ascending. Use '-order' for descending.
    depth: 1,
  })

  return docs
}
```

If you're using the REST API or fetch directly:

```typescript
// app/(frontend)/page.tsx  or similar
const res = await fetch(
  `${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SERVER_URL}/api/case-studies?sort=order&depth=1`,
  { next: { tags: ['case-studies'] } }   // ISR cache tag
)
```

### Step 4 — Lock the component so it never re-sorts

If there is also a hardcoded array anywhere (the AP-034 variant), replace it:

```typescript
// components/home/CaseStudyGrid.tsx
// ❌ BEFORE — magic array, order is whoever last edited this file
const CASE_STUDIES = [
  { slug: 'lacework', ... },
  { slug: 'design-system', ... },
  { slug: 'goldman-sachs', ... },
]

// ✅ AFTER — order comes from CMS, component is order-agnostic
interface Props {
  caseStudies: CaseStudy[]   // already sorted by query
}

export function CaseStudyGrid({ caseStudies }: Props) {
  return (
    <section>
      {caseStudies.map((study) => (
        <CaseStudyCard key={study.id} study={study} />
      ))}
    </section>
  )
}
```

---

## Documentation — Project Rule `RULE-ORDER-001`

Create this file and commit it alongside the fix:

```markdown
<!-- docs/rules/RULE-ORDER-001-case-study-order.md -->

# RULE-ORDER-001 · Case Study Display Order

## Canonical Order (as of [today's date])
| Position | Title          | `order` field value |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1st      | Goldman Sachs  | 1                   |
| 2nd      | Design System  | 2                   |
| 3rd      | Lacework       | 3                   |

## How Order Is Controlled
- Defined by the `order` (Number) field on the `case-studies` Payload collection.
- All queries MUST include `sort: 'order'`. Never omit the sort parameter.
- The home-page component (`CaseStudyGrid`) is intentionally order-agnostic;
  it renders whatever the query returns. Do NOT reorder inside the component.

## Anti-Patterns This Rule Prevents
- **AP-072** – Implicit ordering by insertion/update time (missing `sort`).
- **AP-034** – Magic array order hardcoded in a React component.

## Changing the Order
1. Update the `order` field values in Payload Admin (or seed script).
2. Update the table above in this file in the same PR.
3. No code changes required.

## Why Goldman Sachs Is First
Goldman Sachs is the highest-signal enterprise case study and should anchor
the portfolio. This is a deliberate editorial decision by the site owner.
Never change this without an explicit instruction.
```

---

## Principle

> **Presentation order is editorial content, not code.**
> It belongs in the CMS with an explicit numeric field, surfaced by an explicit `sort` parameter in every query. A component that renders a list must be order-agnostic — it trusts the data layer, not its own internal array. Any time a query omits `sort`, the order is undefined and will eventually be wrong. (AP-072)
